text: Illustration of a mixing vessel where one cycle of the separation and recovery process of the TALSPEAK process takes place. The TALSPEAK Process for actinide/lanthanide separation was originally developed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in the 1960s by B. Weaver and F.A. Kappelmann. This process was developed to act as a substitute for the TRAMEX process that involved selective extraction of the trivalent actinides using a tertiary or quaternary amine which extracted them preferentially from a concentrated LiCl solution when compared to the fission product lanthanides. In the TALSPEAK process the trivalent lanthanides are selectively extracted away from the actinides that are retained in the aqueous phase in the form of aminopolycarboxylate complexes. Despite several decades of process development work on the TALSPEAK concept the conventional version of the TALSPEAK process – utilizing di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (HDEHP) extractant in 1,4-di-isopropylbenzene (DIBP) diluent along with a concentrated lactate buffer that contains a trace amount of diethylenetriamine-N,N,N',N",N"-pentaacetic acid (DTPA) as the actinide selective holdback reagent – has proven to be the benchmark process and concept for advanced aqueous partitioning of the trivalent 4f and 5f elements and TALSPEAK “chemistry” has also been utilized in the so-called “reverse TALSPEAK” process with notable success. The conventional TALSPEAK separation concept has demonstrated under ideal separation conditions a separation factor between Am<sup>3+</sup> and Nd<sup>3+</sup> (the limiting pair of interest in most actinide recycling programs) of about 100 with more effective separation between the other elements and Am<sup>3+</sup> being also demonstrated as well. However, TALSPEAK has not been adopted with great fervor as a process for advancement of nuclear fuel resource recovery due to the following two constraints that have developed in process scale demonstrations: i) all process-scale attempts at development of the TALSPEAK Process require that the pH of the aqueous phase be very precisely controlled within a very narrow pH range – of the order of ± 0.1 pH unit, and ii) the phase transfer kinetics as generally observed to be slower-than-ideal. As a consequence of these constraints, high concentrations of buffer in the aqueous phase need to be maintained at values of from 0.5 to 2.0 M H/Na lactate. Research which has established the fundamental chemistry of TALSPEAK that has been carried out over the last decade, along with discussions of the implications and constraints and related to its application and enhancement in process development and improvement will be discussed in the following report.
explanation: The text uses domain-specific terminology and plausible historical notes typical of technical reporting, with no obvious AI-generation artifacts. However, such content can be produced by both humans and AI, so the likelihood of AI origin is uncertain.
